# Part 7 — The Bluetooth Module {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #00C9A7)"}

## Cutting the Cord

Instead of an infrared remote, we are going to use **Bluetooth**! This means we can control our robot using a smartphone app, just like a real RC car or a drone.

We will use an **HM-10** Bluetooth module and the **Dabble App**, which transforms your smartphone into a virtual controller.

> 🔗 **Explore:** [Dabble - Your Smartphone as a Controller](https://thestempedia.com/product/dabble/)

## Installing the Dabble Library

Before we can write our code, we need to add the Dabble library to the Arduino IDE:

> 📥 **Download:** [Dabble-Arduino.zip](https://thestempedia.com/download/28488/)

In the Arduino IDE, go to **Sketch** → **Include Library** → **Add .ZIP Library...** and select the file you just downloaded.

![Adding the Library](https://ai.thestempedia.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Library-Installation.png)


# Part 7 — App Driving {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #00C9A7)"}

## The Dabble Gamepad

We will use the **Gamepad Module** in the Dabble app to drive our robot. The Gamepad has buttons like Up, Down, Left, and Right.

![Dabble Gamepad Module](https://ai.thestempedia.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/Game-Pad.jpg)

## Complete Bluetooth Code

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}
**What's New?**

1. **Include Dabble:** 
   `#include <Dabble.h>` and `Dabble.begin(Serial)`
2. **Read Gamepad:** 
   `Dabble.processInput()` and `GamePad.isUpPressed()`

*The movement functions (`car_front()`, etc.) remain exactly the same as the IR remote!*
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}

```cpp {code-line-numbers="1-3,18-19,22-38,41"}
#define CUSTOM_SETTINGS
#define INCLUDE_GAMEPAD_MODULE
#include <Dabble.h>

#define ML_Ctrl 4    
#define ML_PWM 5   
#define MR_Ctrl 2    
#define MR_PWM 6   

int carSpeed = 100; 

void setup() {
    pinMode(ML_Ctrl, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(ML_PWM, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(MR_Ctrl, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(MR_PWM, OUTPUT);
    
    Serial.begin(9600);
    Dabble.begin(Serial); // Start Bluetooth using Hardware Serial
}

void handleCommand() {
    if (GamePad.isUpPressed()) {
        car_front();
    } 
    else if (GamePad.isDownPressed()) {
        car_back();
    }
    else if (GamePad.isLeftPressed()) {
        car_left();
    }
    else if (GamePad.isRightPressed()) {
        car_right();
    }
    else {
        car_Stop();
    }
}

void loop() {
    Dabble.processInput(); // Refresh data from the smartphone
    handleCommand();
}

// --- Movement Functions ---
. . .

```
:::
::::

## Upload Code Before Wiring!

::: {.callout-important}
### ⚠️ STOP & UPLOAD! 

Because the Bluetooth module uses **Pins 0 and 1** (TX/RX), it will **block** your computer from uploading code.

1. **Keep the Bluetooth module disconnected.**
2. **Upload** the code from the previous slide.
3. **ONLY THEN** proceed to the wiring step.

*If you need to upload again later, you must disconnect TX/RX wires first!*
:::

**👉 Action Item:** Upload the code to your Arduino Uno right now.

## Wiring the Module

::: {.callout-warning icon=false}
**⚠️ Caution:** **Do NOT swap VCC and GND!** Reversing these pins can permanently damage your Bluetooth module.
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](assets/BT_connect.png)

1. **VCC** ➔ Arduino `5V`  
2. **GND** ➔ Arduino `GND`  
3. **TXD** ➔ Arduino **RX (Pin 0)**  
4. **RXD** ➔ Arduino **TX (Pin 1)**  
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](assets/BT_kit.png){height=50% width=60%}

:::

::::

## The Driving Challenge

Now it's time to test! 

1. Disconnect the USB cable from the Arduino (very important!).
2. Turn on the battery pack.
3. Open the Dabble app on your smartphone.
4. Click the connect icon and pair it with your HM-10 module (Password: `1234` or `000000`).
5. Select the **Gamepad** module and drive!
